content.title: 2016 ACC/AHA Guideline Focused Update on Duration of Dual Antiplatelet Therapy in Patients With Coronary Artery Disease
content.organization: American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association Task Force on Clinical Practice Guidelines
content.context: This update focuses on the duration of dual antiplatelet therapy for patients with coronary artery disease. The guidelines are built on a series of previous guidelines covering a wide array of cardiovascular conditions and procedures.
content.collaboration: Developed in collaboration with the American Association for Thoracic Surgery, American Society of Anesthesiologists, Society for Cardiovascular Angiography and Interventions, Society of Cardiovascular Anesthesiologists, and Society of Thoracic Surgeons.
content.endorsement: Endorsed by Preventive Cardiovascular Nurses Association and Society for Vascular Surgery.
content.additional information: Focused Update writing group members have disclosed their industry associations, details of which are available in Appendix 1.

table.title: Applying Class of Recommendation and Level of Evidence to Clinical Strategies, Interventions, Treatments, or Diagnostic Testing in Patient Care

suggested_phrases: Treatment/strategy A is recommended/indicated in preference to treatment B
Treatment A should be chosen over treatment B
Treatment/strategy A is probably recommended/indicated in preference to treatment B
It is reasonable to choose treatment A over treatment B
Usefulness/effectiveness is unknown/unclear/uncertain or not well established

levels_of_evidence.description: High-quality evidence from more than 1 RCT, meta-analyses of high-quality RCTs, or one or more RCTs corroborated by high-quality registry studies
Moderate-quality evidence from 1 or more RCTs, meta-analyses of moderate-quality RCTs
Moderate-quality evidence from 1 or more well-designed, well-executed nonrandomized studies, observational studies, or registry studies
Randomized or nonrandomized observational or registry studies with limitations of design or execution, meta-analyses of such studies
Consensus of expert opinion based on clinical experience

content.guidelines_review: Guidelines are reviewed annually by the Task Force and are official policy of the ACC and AHA.

content.introduction: The guideline focuses on the duration of dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) involving aspirin plus a P2Y12 inhibitor for patients with coronary artery disease (CAD). The focus is on updates to recommendations across six existing ACCF/AHA guidelines. This document reviews 11 studies involving patients treated with DES and assesses the impact of different DAPT durations. The goal is to evaluate, update, harmonize, and simplify recommendations on DAPT duration.
content.methodology: The guideline is based on systematic reviews, existing recommendation evaluations, and includes an evidence review using PICOTS format targeting intervention, comparison, outcome, timing, and setting. It describes various factors influencing these recommendations, including information from over 11 studies and other related clinical trials.
content.context: Focus is primarily on recommendations related to PCI, CABG, and patients with chronic conditions post-acute coronary events.

content.Antiplatelet Therapy in Patients with Co: The document discusses focused updates on the duration of dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) in patients with coronary artery disease. Key updates are drawn from 6 clinical practice guidelines involving cardiovascular and internal medicine experts. The document addresses duration of DAPT following drug-eluting stent (DES) implantation, comparing shorter (3 to 6 months) and longer durations (18 to 48 months). Evidence indicates shorter DAPT duration results in fewer bleeding complications, and may be optimal with newer-generation DES that are less likely to cause thrombosis. Prolonged DAPT potentially reduces late stent thrombosis and adverse events, though also associated with increased bleeding risk.

content.extended_dapt: Studies of longer-duration DAPT found an absolute decrease in late stent thrombosis and ischemic complications of ≈1% to 2% and an absolute increase in bleeding complications of ≈1%. RCTs showed extended DAPT decreased absolute risk of MACE by 1.1% and major bleeding by 0.8%.
content.charisma: The CHARISMA trial showed no significant reduction in ischemic events with DAPT and a 0.4% increase in severe bleeding. DAPT with clopidogrel showed an absolute 1.3% reduction in cardiovascular events but increased major bleeding by 1.2%.
content.pegasus-timi 54: The study found a 1.2% reduction in cardiovascular death, MI, or stroke and 1.2% increase in major bleeding with DAPT. Reduction in ischemic events occurred with patients not on P2Y12 inhibitors or discontinued for ≤30 days.
content.prolonged_dapt: An unexpected increase in all-cause mortality rate by 0.5% was found, particularly from noncardiovascular deaths in the DAPT study. META analysis and the FDA communication indicated no significant increase in mortality risks for clopidogrel.

genes.context: Benefit in patients when P2Y12 inhibitor therapy either had not been discontinued or had been discontinued for ≤30 days.

content.Overview: The guidelines discuss the concepts and recommendations for Dual Antiplatelet Therapy (DAPT) and its duration. Emphasis is placed on the balance between reducing ischemic risk and increasing bleeding risk with the use of P2Y12 inhibitors in addition to aspirin. Recommendations for DAPT duration are presented, with a focus on individualized assessment based on clinical judgment, patient preferences, and risk factors.
content.Duration Recommendations: A Class I recommendation is made for a minimum of 6 to 12 months of DAPT for most patients. Extension beyond this period may be suggested for certain patients, depending on individual risk factors and preferences.
content.Considerations: Shorter-duration DAPT is suitable for patients at lower ischemic risk with higher bleeding risk, whereas longer-duration DAPT is preferrable for patients at higher ischemic risk with lower bleeding risk. Use of ticagrelor or prasugrel is suggested over clopidogrel for decreased ischemic risk and improved outcomes.
content.Bleeding Risk: Factors associated with increased ischemic and bleeding risk include prior PCI, congestive heart failure, ejection fraction, diabetes mellitus, and stent characteristics.
content.Tools: The DAPT score helps evaluate the benefit/risk ratio of prolonged DAPT. Higher scores (≥2) may favor prolonged DAPT due to the net reduction of ischemic events.

genes.context: Intensification of antiplatelet therapy, with the addition of a P2Y12 inhibitor to aspirin monotherapy, lowers ischemic risk but increases bleeding risk. Recommendations suggest that decisions about treatment duration of DAPT require consideration of P2Y12 inhibitor therapy.

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with ACS (NSTE-ACS or STEMI) treated with DAPT after coronary stent implantation and in patients with NSTE-ACS treated with medical therapy alone (without revascularization), it is reasonable to use ticagrelor in preference to clopidogrel for maintenance P2Y12 inhibitor therapy.

In patients with ACS (NSTE-ACS or STEMI) treated with DAPT after coronary stent implantation who are not at high risk for bleeding complications and do not have a history of stroke or TIA, it is reasonable to choose prasugrel over clopidogrel for maintenance P2Y12 inhibitor therapy.
Prasugrel should not be administered to patients with a prior history of stroke or TIA.

platelet_function_testing.context: The role of platelet function testing and genetic testing in patients treated with DAPT is addressed in the 2011 ACCF/AHA/SCAI PCI guideline and the 2014 ACC/AHA NSTE-ACS guideline. No RCT has demonstrated that routine platelet function testing or genetic testing to guide P2Y12 inhibitor therapy improves outcome.

proton_pump_inhibitors.context: The use of proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) in patients treated with DAPT is discussed, with PPIs recommended for patients with a history of prior gastrointestinal bleeding, and the routine use of PPIs in patients at low risk of gastrointestinal bleeding is not recommended.

content.aspirin_dosing_recommendation: In patients treated with DAPT, a daily aspirin dose of 81 mg (range, 75 mg to 100 mg) is recommended.
content.study_findings: Studies of nearly 200,000 persons have shown that lower aspirin doses (≤100 mg daily) are associated with less major and total bleeding than higher doses.
content.P2Y12_inhibitor_findings: The efficacy of ticagrelor seems to be decreased in patients treated with higher aspirin doses (≥300 mg daily) versus lower aspirin doses (≤100 mg daily).
content.optimal_aspirin_dosing: The optimal range of aspirin dose in patients treated with DAPT for maximal protection is 75 mg to 100 mg.

clinical_guidelines.title: Focused Update on Duration of Dual Antiplatelet Therapy
clinical_guidelines.Recommendations on triple therapy: Beyond the scope but included for completeness and end-user education.
clinical_guidelines.4.1 Duration of DAPT in Patients With SI: See Online Data Supplements 1 to 3 and 6 to 9 for evidence.

Recommendations for Duration of DAPT in.Recommendation: In patients with SIHD treated with DAPT after BMS implantation, P2Y12 inhibitor therapy (clopidogrel) should be given for a minimum of 1 month.
In patients with SIHD treated with DAPT after DES implantation, P2Y12 inhibitor therapy (clopidogrel) should be given for at least 6 months.
In patients treated with DAPT, a daily aspirin dose of 81 mg (range, 75 mg to 100 mg) is recommended.

In patients with SIHD treated with DAPT after BMS or DES implantation who have tolerated DAPT without a bleeding complication and who are not at high risk of bleeding, continuation of DAPT with clopidogrel for longer than 1 month in patients treated with BMS or longer than 6 months in patients treated with DES may be reasonable.
In patients with SIHD treated with DAPT after DES implantation who develop a high risk of bleeding, discontinuation of P2Y12 inhibitor therapy after 3 months may be reasonable.

Duration of DAPT in Patients With ACS Tr.Recommendation: In patients with ACS (NSTE-ACS or STEMI) treated with DAPT after BMS or DES implantation, P2Y12 inhibitor therapy (clopidogrel, prasugrel, or ticagrelor) should be given for at least 12 months.
In patients treated with DAPT, a daily aspirin dose of 81 mg (range, 75 mg to 100 mg) is recommended.
In patients with ACS (NSTE-ACS or STEMI) treated with DAPT after coronary stent implantation, it is reasonable to use ticagrelor in preference to clopidogrel for maintenance P2Y12 inhibitor therapy.

In patients with ACS (NSTE-ACS or STEMI) treated with DAPT after coronary stent implantation who are not at high risk for bleeding complications and who do not have a history of stroke or TIA, it is reasonable to choose prasugrel over clopidogrel.

In patients with ACS (NSTE-ACS or STEMI) treated with coronary stent implantation who have tolerated DAPT without a bleeding complication and who are not at high bleeding risk, continuation of DAPT for longer than 12 months may be reasonable.
In patients with ACS treated with DAPT after DES implantation who develop a high risk of severe bleeding, discontinuation of P2Y12 inhibitor therapy after 6 months may be reasonable.
Prasugrel should not be administered to patients with a prior history of stroke or TIA.

content.section: 4.3. Duration of DAPT in Patients With SIHD and ACS Treated With PCI
content.summary: The guideline discusses the recommended duration of dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) in patients treated with coronary stent implantation. It emphasizes the higher risk of stent thrombosis shortly after implantation, particularly with bare metal stents (BMS). A minimum duration of DAPT of 1 month is typically recommended for BMS patients. Drug-eluting stents (DES) have varying recommended durations owing to differences in stent generation, with newer generation stents requiring shorter durations. The document includes a treatment algorithm outlining recommendations based on bleeding risks and stent types.

content.thrombosis: Shorter-duration DAPT reduces thrombosis in patients with SIHD treated with DES. Minimum recommended duration reduced from 12 to 6 months.
content.PCI-CURE analysis: Suggested continuing DAPT for up to 12 months in NSTE-ACS patients treated with BMS reduced ischemic events.
content.Extended DAPT: Extended DAPT for 18 to 36 months can reduce ischemic complications but increases bleeding risks.
content.Bleeding Risk: Higher DAPT score may favor prolonged DAPT; lower may not.
content.Drug-eluting stents: Newer stents like everolimus-eluting stents show reduced thrombosis, modest benefit in prolonged DAPT.
content.Bioabsorbable stents: Protocol may require continuation for 6 to 12 months.
content.CABG recommendations: Includes resuming DAPT post-CABG to complete 12 months DAPT therapy.

Aspirin therapy after CABG.Improves vein graft patency: Particularly during the first postoperative year
Aspirin therapy after CABG.Reduces MACE: According to studies such as CURE
Aspirin therapy after CABG.Comparison with clopidogrel: Patients treated with aspirin plus clopidogrel showed significant reduction in ischemic events

content.Propensity score analysis: In a Danish administrative database showed fewer deaths with aspirin plus clopidogrel

Impact of clopidogrel on graft occlusion.Evaluated in 5 studies: Did not demonstrate significant differences between monotherapy and DAPT

Treatment Algorithm for P2Y12 Inhibitor.Class IIb recommendation: 12 months of clopidogrel may be reasonable
Treatment Algorithm for P2Y12 Inhibitor.Class I recommendation: Resume P2Y12 inhibitor post-op to complete 12 months of DAPT

content.clinical_guideline:
This guideline provides recommendations for the duration of dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) in patients with stable ischemic heart disease (SIHD). Key recommendations include:

1. For patients treated with bare metal stents (BMS) using DAPT with clopidogrel, therapy should be continued for a minimum of 1 month.
2. For patients treated with drug-eluting stents (DES) using DAPT with clopidogrel, therapy should extend for at least 6 months.
3. A daily aspirin dose of 81 mg is recommended for patients on DAPT.
4. Continuous DAPT is recommended for specific high-risk patients.
5. Discontinuation of P2Y12 inhibitor therapy may be reasonable for patients at high risk of bleeding after DES implantation.

content.title: Treatment Algorithm for Duration of P2Y12 Inhibitor Therapy in Patients with SIHD
content.description: This guideline focuses on the duration of dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) in patients with stable ischemic heart disease (SIHD) without acute coronary syndrome (ACS) within the past several years. It highlights the use of P2Y12 inhibitors and aspirin therapy, providing guidance based on levels of bleeding risk, history of myocardial infarction (MI), and other clinical scenarios. It is recommended to consider patient history, potential bleeding risks, and benefit-risk ratios when determining the duration of DAPT.
key points: P2Y12 inhibitor therapy is recommended for varying durations depending on the history of ACS, MI, or stent implementation.
In patients with no prior MI or recent coronary intervention, continuing P2Y12 inhibitors is generally not beneficial.
In patients with prior MI, longer durations of P2Y12 inhibitor therapy may be reasonable based on individual risk assessments.
High bleeding risk requires careful consideration of the duration and necessity of continued therapy.

genes.context: Clopidogrel is the only currently used P2Y12 inhibitor studied in patients with SIHD undergoing PCI. The greatest reduction in ischemic events was seen in patients in whom P2Y12 inhibitor therapy either had not been discontinued or had been discontinued ≤30 days before enrollment in the study.

recommendations.condition: ACS treated with medical therapy alone

guidelines.recommendation: Continue DAPT with P2Y12 inhibitor therapy (clopidogrel or ticagrelor) for at least 12 months.
Daily aspirin dose of 81 mg is recommended.
Prefer ticagrelor over clopidogrel for maintenance of P2Y12 inhibitor therapy.
Continuation of DAPT for longer than 12 months may be reasonable in patients without high bleeding risk.
Continue P2Y12 inhibitor therapy (clopidogrel) for a minimum of 14 days, ideally at least 12 months.
Daily aspirin dose of 81 mg is recommended.
Continuation of DAPT for longer than 12 months may be reasonable in patients without high bleeding risk.

sections.section: 7.3. Duration of DAPT in Patients With ACS Treated With PCI: Recommendations

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with ACS treated with DAPT after BMS or DES implantation, P2Y12 inhibitor therapy (clopidogrel, prasugrel, or ticagrelor) should be given for at least 12 months.
In patients treated with DAPT, a daily aspirin dose of 81 mg (range, 75 mg to 100 mg) is recommended.
In patients with ACS treated with DAPT after coronary stent implantation, it is reasonable to use ticagrelor in preference to clopidogrel for maintenance P2Y12 inhibitor therapy.

In patients with ACS treated with DAPT after coronary stent implantation, who are not at high risk for bleeding complications and who do not have a history of stroke or TIA, it is reasonable to choose prasugrel over clopidogrel for maintenance P2Y12 inhibitor therapy.

In patients with ACS treated with coronary stent implantation who have tolerated DAPT without bleeding complications and who are not at high bleeding risk, continuation of DAPT for longer than 12 months may be reasonable.
In patients with ACS treated with DAPT after DES implantation who develop a high risk of bleeding or significant overt bleeding, discontinuation of P2Y12 therapy after 6 months may be reasonable.
Prasugrel should not be administered to patients with a prior history of stroke or TIA.

sections.section: 7.4. Duration of DAPT in Patients With ACS Treated With CABG: Recommendation

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with ACS being treated with DAPT who undergo CABG, P2Y12 inhibitor therapy should be resumed after CABG to complete 12 months of DAPT therapy after ACS.

sections.section: 7.5. Duration of DAPT in Patients With ACS
sections.content: Aspirin remains the cornerstone of antiplatelet therapy in patients with ACS. Further platelet inhibition can be achieved by blocking the P2Y12 receptor.

content.summary: The guideline discusses the recommended duration and considerations for Dual Antiplatelet Therapy (DAPT) in patients with Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) and those undergoing Percutaneous Coronary Intervention (PCI). It emphasizes the balance between reducing ischemic risks and increasing bleeding risks, especially post-myocardial infarction (MI). The document also outlines recommendations for elective noncardiac surgery timing in patients treated with PCI and DAPT, highlighting the need for careful assessment of the patient's individual situation and preferences.
clinical_recommendations: DAPT is recommended for at least 12 months in patients treated with PCI without revascularization or reperfusion therapy.
Short-term treatment with clopidogrel is beneficial in STEMI patients not managed with primary PCI.
Prolonged DAPT (beyond 12 months) should be carefully considered, balancing ischemic and bleeding risks.
Elective noncardiac surgery should generally be delayed for specific timeframes after stent implantation to minimize risks.

surgery_timing_recommendations.recommendation: Elective noncardiac surgery should be delayed 30 days after BMS implantation and optimally 6 months after DES implantation.
Aspirin should be continued if possible and P2Y12 inhibitor restarted post-surgery in patients who had to discontinue DAPT due to surgery.
Risk assessment is crucial for patients undergoing surgery who are on a P2Y12 inhibitor.
Elective surgery after DES implantation may be considered 3 months post-implant if surgical risks are lower than stent thrombosis risk.
Noncardiac surgery should be avoided within 30 days after BMS or 3 months after DES if DAPT discontinuation is required.

content.Title: Focused Update on Duration of Dual Antiplatelet Therapy
content.Guideline Summary: The guideline provides a treatment algorithm for the duration of P2Y12 inhibitor therapy in patients with recent acute coronary syndrome (ACS), including both non-ST-elevation ACS (NSTE-ACS) and ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI). The algorithm recommends varying durations based on the type of medical intervention post-ACS, such as CABG, medical therapy, lytic therapy, or PCI. For each case, different inhibitors like clopidogrel, prasugrel, or ticagrelor are suggested with a duration of at least 12 months, with considerations for discontinuation in the presence of high bleeding risk.
content.Considerations for Noncardiac Surgery: For patients with coronary stents, the risk of stent thrombosis and bleeding if dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) is interrupted are critical factors. DAPT significantly reduces the risk of stent thrombosis. The bleeding risk is highest in the first 4 to 6 weeks after stent implantation. Elevated risk persists even a year after DES placement.

Classifications.Class I: Strongly recommends specific durations of therapy in various scenarios, such as after CABG or PCI.
Classifications.Class IIb: Indicates that discontinuation may be reasonable after 6 or 12 months, contingent on bleeding risks.

content.Summary: The clinical guideline addresses the timing of elective noncardiac surgery in patients with coronary stents, specifically those treated with dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) and drug-eluting stents (DES). The guideline highlights differences in recommendations based on the duration since stent implantation. For bare-metal stents (BMS) implanted less than 30 days ago, delaying surgery is advised (Class III: Harm), while for those implanted more than 30 days ago, surgery may proceed (Class I). For DES, surgery should be delayed for less than 3 months post-implantation (Class III: Harm) and may be considered after 3-6 months (Class IIb). Surgery is recommended after 6 months post-implantation (Class I). Newer-generation DES is associated with lower stent thrombosis risk and shorter DAPT durations.

content.text: If a P2Y12 inhibitor has been held before a surgical procedure, therapy is restarted as soon as possible, given the substantial thrombotic hazard associated with lack of platelet inhibition early after surgery in patients with recent stent implantation. Although several small studies have used intravenous antiplatelet agents as a means of 'bridging' in patients requiring temporary discontinuation of DAPT before surgery, there is no convincing clinical evidence demonstrating the efficacy of bridging with either parenteral antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy. Decisions about the timing of surgery and whether to discontinue DAPT after coronary stent implantation are best individualized. Such decisions involve weighing the particular surgical procedure and the risks of delaying the procedure, the risks of ischemia and stent thrombosis, and the risk and consequences of bleeding. Given the complexity of these considerations, decisions are best determined by a consensus of the surgeon, anesthesiologist, cardiologist, and patient.

content.title: Focused Update on Duration of Dual Antiplatelet Therapy
content.introduction: This guideline provides updates on the duration of dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) in patients, particularly those with coronary artery disease undergoing stent placements or other interventions.
key_topics: The optimal duration of DAPT following various types of coronary interventions.
Comparisons between newer and older stent technologies in terms of DAPT requirements.
Impact of DAPT duration on mortality and cardiovascular events.
Guidance from various clinical trials and meta-analyses.
clinical_recommendations: Patients post-myocardial infarction may benefit from longer durations of DAPT.
The risks associated with prolonged DAPT, especially regarding bleeding.
content.conclusion: The guideline stresses individualized decision-making based on patient risk profiles, stent types, and clinical scenarios.

content.title: Focused Update on Duration of Dual Antiplatelet Therapy
content.focus: The document primarily focuses on guidelines related to the duration of dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT). It covers a variety of studies and trials that explore DAPT in the context of coronary stent thrombosis, acute coronary syndromes, myocardial infarction, and other cardiovascular conditions. Key studies and trials referenced include the PLATO trial, CURE study, and analysis from the ACC/AHA regarding antithrombotic therapy, among others.

General Information: Discusses various studies and trials related to antiplatelet therapy, particularly after coronary stent placement.
Comparison of drug-eluting stents with bioresorbable scaffolds in terms of efficacy and safety.
Studies on the timing and discontinuation effects of antiplatelet therapies post-coronary surgeries.
Risks associated with clopidogrel and aspirin in acute coronary syndrome patients.
Long-term outcomes and comparisons between different stent types and antiplatelet regimens.
Evaluation of drug-coated balloons and bioresorbable stents.

content.title: Focused Update on Duration of Dual Antiplatelet Therapy
content.summary: The document discusses clinical guidelines and findings related to dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT), its duration, and the implications for patients undergoing various cardiac and non-cardiac surgical procedures. It covers factors influencing coronary artery bypass grafting, drug-eluting stents, and strategies for reducing cardiovascular risks. The guidelines also explore perioperative management and outcomes associated with different antiplatelet strategies.
key_points: Evaluation of DAPT duration for patients with drug-eluting stents.
Impact of discontinuation and reintroduction of antithrombotic therapies in surgery settings.
Risk assessments for bleeding and thrombotic events.
Role of P2Y12 receptor inhibitors and aspirin in perioperative scenarios.
references: Guidelines for bleeding risk assessment in surgeries.
Meta-analyses on DAPT in non-cardiac surgeries.

content.disclaimer: This table represents the relationships of reviewers with industry and other entities that were disclosed at the time of peer review and determined to be relevant to this document. It does not necessarily reflect relationships with industry at the time of publication. A person is deemed to have a significant interest in a business if the interest represents ownership of  ≥5% of the voting stock or share of the business entity, or ownership of ≥$5,000 of the fair market value of the business entity, or if funds received by the person from the business entity exceed 5% of the person's gross income for the previous year. A relationship is considered to be modest if it is less than significant under the preceding definition. Relationships that exist with no financial benefit are also included for the purpose of transparency.

context: Benefit in patients when P2Y12 inhibitor therapy either had not been discontinued or had been discontinued for ≤30 days.

Intensification of antiplatelet therapy, with the addition of a P2Y12 inhibitor to aspirin monotherapy, lowers ischemic risk but increases bleeding risk. Recommendations suggest that decisions about treatment duration of DAPT require consideration of P2Y12 inhibitor therapy.

Clopidogrel is the only currently used P2Y12 inhibitor studied in patients with SIHD undergoing PCI. The greatest reduction in ischemic events was seen in patients in whom P2Y12 inhibitor therapy either had not been discontinued or had been discontinued ≤30 days before enrollment in the study.
